FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional baseline (2016-2018) data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, n=10,755, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, ages 9-10 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis; multiple linear regression estimating associations between sociodemographic factors and six screen time modalities.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in The Journal of Pediatrics (2022); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social networking, texting, and video chat each have separate regression coefficients in Table III. However, the only variables on the other side of every analysis are sociodemographic predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parental education). Demographic-only predictors do not qualify as individual-trait-level per FT-IC2 other-side rule. No individual-trait-level variable (health, behavioral, cognitive, well-being, etc.) appears on either side of any analysis. FT-IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: Paper is purely a sociodemographic epidemiology of screen time paper; IVs are all demographic, DVs are screen time modalities including SM/texting/video chat. Other side of the SM relationship is demographic-only throughout.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, December 2020 COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release; N=4,106 U.S. adolescents ages 12-14.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using modified Poisson regression on December 2020 COVID-19 survey data from ABCD.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Preventive Medicine Reports (2022); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Checking COVID-related social media (e.g., Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat) is an IV with a separate numeric estimate (ARR 1.09, 95% CI 1.00-1.19, p=0.049) predicting 'always masking' behavior (Table 2). Masking behavior is a health protective behavior -- an individual-trait-level health outcome. SM variable is the IV; health behavior is the DV. IC2 = MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression reports adjusted risk ratios (ARR) with 95% CIs and p-values for the social media → masking relationship (ARR 1.09, 95% CI 1.00-1.19, p=0.049).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported (ARRs, 95% CIs, p-values) for the SM-masking relationship.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (December 2020 survey); SM is IV (not DV-only). EC7 does not apply.
notes: Social media is measured as frequency of checking COVID-related information on social media, used as IV predicting masking behavior. Cross-sectional analysis only.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from ABCD Study Year 2 (2018-2020, 4.0 release); N=8,753; 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis; multiple linear regression estimating sociodemographic correlates of problematic video game, social media, and mobile phone use.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatric Research (2022); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The DVs across all analyses are VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), and MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire). Per binding clarification, SMAQ and MPIQ are SM/phone variables, not individual-trait outcomes on the other side. The IVs are exclusively sociodemographic (age, sex, race/ethnicity, primary language, household income, parental education, parent marital status) -- demographic-only predictors. No analysis pairs a qualifying SM/phone variable with an individual-trait-level variable on the other side. The only analyses are demographics → SMAQ/MPIQ/VGAQ. FT-IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: All three DVs (VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ) are SM/phone variables per binding clarifications. IVs are all sociodemographic (demographic-only). No individual-trait-level variable appears on either side of any analysis.
